title.de: IAA Exam Deep Detail Flashcards — Non-Duplicate Set
title.en: IAA Exam Deep Detail Flashcards — Non-Duplicate Set

question.de: Why can an impossible but numeric value be more dangerous than a missing value?
question.en: Why can an impossible but numeric value be more dangerous than a missing value?

answer.de:
• A missing value is usually visible during preprocessing
• An impossible numeric value may pass type checks and look usable to the model
• Examples include negative blood pressure, purchase amount = -999, or impossible age values
• The model may interpret the value as a real signal instead of an error
• Domain knowledge is needed to detect and correct such entries
answer.en:
• A missing value is usually visible during preprocessing
• An impossible numeric value may pass type checks and look usable to the model
• Examples include negative blood pressure, purchase amount = -999, or impossible age values
• The model may interpret the value as a real signal instead of an error
• Domain knowledge is needed to detect and correct such entries

question.de: How can information from different data sources reveal incorrect entries?
question.en: How can information from different data sources reveal incorrect entries?

answer.de:
• Measurements of the same phenomenon can be compared across sources
• Inconsistencies may indicate sensor errors, pipeline problems, or annotation mistakes
• Example: heart rate estimated from ECG should be compatible with heart rate estimated from plethysmography
• The goal is not only to delete inconsistent rows but to understand which source is unreliable
• This is a data-cleaning task that depends strongly on domain knowledge
answer.en:
• Measurements of the same phenomenon can be compared across sources
• Inconsistencies may indicate sensor errors, pipeline problems, or annotation mistakes
• Example: heart rate estimated from ECG should be compatible with heart rate estimated from plethysmography
• The goal is not only to delete inconsistent rows but to understand which source is unreliable
• This is a data-cleaning task that depends strongly on domain knowledge

question.de: Why can wrapper feature selection overfit more easily than filter feature selection?
question.en: Why can wrapper feature selection overfit more easily than filter feature selection?

answer.de:
• Wrapper methods evaluate feature subsets using model performance
• They repeatedly search many possible feature combinations
• This can adapt too closely to the validation data or cross-validation splits
• The selected subset may look optimal only for the sampled data
• Filter methods are less model-specific and cheaper, but may miss feature interactions
answer.en:
• Wrapper methods evaluate feature subsets using model performance
• They repeatedly search many possible feature combinations
• This can adapt too closely to the validation data or cross-validation splits
• The selected subset may look optimal only for the sampled data
• Filter methods are less model-specific and cheaper, but may miss feature interactions

question.de: Why can univariate filter methods miss important predictors?
question.en: Why can univariate filter methods miss important predictors?

answer.de:
• Univariate filters evaluate each feature separately
• A feature may look weak alone but become useful together with another feature
• Interactions between predictors are ignored
• Correlated predictors can also appear individually important even if they carry redundant information
• This is why filter scores should be combined with domain knowledge and validation
answer.en:
• Univariate filters evaluate each feature separately
• A feature may look weak alone but become useful together with another feature
• Interactions between predictors are ignored
• Correlated predictors can also appear individually important even if they carry redundant information
• This is why filter scores should be combined with domain knowledge and validation

question.de: Why can biased sampling sometimes be intentional rather than a mistake?
question.en: Why can biased sampling sometimes be intentional rather than a mistake?

answer.de:
• Some regions of the data may be more relevant to the scientific or business question
• Rare events may need to be oversampled to be represented sufficiently
• In anomaly or failure detection, the rare cases can be more important than normal cases
• Intentional biased sampling must be documented because it changes the training distribution
• Evaluation should still reflect the real deployment distribution when possible
answer.en:
• Some regions of the data may be more relevant to the scientific or business question
• Rare events may need to be oversampled to be represented sufficiently
• In anomaly or failure detection, the rare cases can be more important than normal cases
• Intentional biased sampling must be documented because it changes the training distribution
• Evaluation should still reflect the real deployment distribution when possible

question.de: Why can the number of histogram bins change the interpretation of a feature distribution?
question.en: Why can the number of histogram bins change the interpretation of a feature distribution?

answer.de:
• Too few bins can hide multimodality, skewness, or local structure
• Too many bins can make random noise look like meaningful structure
• Different bin choices can change whether outliers or gaps appear obvious
• A histogram is therefore exploratory, not definitive evidence
• It should be combined with summary statistics and other plots such as box plots
answer.en:
• Too few bins can hide multimodality, skewness, or local structure
• Too many bins can make random noise look like meaningful structure
• Different bin choices can change whether outliers or gaps appear obvious
• A histogram is therefore exploratory, not definitive evidence
• It should be combined with summary statistics and other plots such as box plots

question.de: Which stopping criteria can be used for K-means and what do they mean?
question.en: Which stopping criteria can be used for K-means and what do they mean?

answer.de:
• Stop after reaching a maximum number of iterations
• Stop when the objective J becomes smaller than a predefined threshold
• Stop when J no longer improves meaningfully between iterations
• Stop when assignments or centroids stop changing
• The criterion should prevent unnecessary computation while avoiding premature stopping
answer.en:
• Stop after reaching a maximum number of iterations
• Stop when the objective J becomes smaller than a predefined threshold
• Stop when J no longer improves meaningfully between iterations
• Stop when assignments or centroids stop changing
• The criterion should prevent unnecessary computation while avoiding premature stopping

question.de: How do Euclidean, Manhattan, and Chebyshev distance differ conceptually?
question.en: How do Euclidean, Manhattan, and Chebyshev distance differ conceptually?

answer.de:
• Euclidean distance measures straight-line distance in feature space
• Manhattan distance sums absolute coordinate-wise differences
• Chebyshev distance uses the largest single coordinate-wise difference
• The chosen distance changes what counts as similar
• Distance choice should match the data geometry and feature meaning
answer.en:
• Euclidean distance measures straight-line distance in feature space
• Manhattan distance sums absolute coordinate-wise differences
• Chebyshev distance uses the largest single coordinate-wise difference
• The chosen distance changes what counts as similar
• Distance choice should match the data geometry and feature meaning

question.de: Why does the centroid update step use the mean of assigned points?
question.en: Why does the centroid update step use the mean of assigned points?

answer.de:
• K-means minimizes squared distances within each cluster
• For squared Euclidean distance, the mean is the point that minimizes the sum of squared deviations
• Updating to the mean therefore reduces or keeps the objective J unchanged
• This is why the algorithm alternates assignment and mean-update steps
• If another distance is used, the mean may no longer be the optimal representative
answer.en:
• K-means minimizes squared distances within each cluster
• For squared Euclidean distance, the mean is the point that minimizes the sum of squared deviations
• Updating to the mean therefore reduces or keeps the objective J unchanged
• This is why the algorithm alternates assignment and mean-update steps
• If another distance is used, the mean may no longer be the optimal representative

question.de: What are the matrix dimensions in the PCA covariance and SVD step for X with m examples and n features?
question.en: What are the matrix dimensions in the PCA covariance and SVD step for X with m examples and n features?

answer.de:
• After mean normalization, X has shape m × n
• The covariance matrix XᵀX / m has shape n × n
• SVD decomposes this covariance matrix into U, S, and V
• U has shape n × n and contains eigenvectors or principal directions
• The first k columns of U form U_reduce with shape n × k
answer.en:
• After mean normalization, X has shape m × n
• The covariance matrix XᵀX / m has shape n × n
• SVD decomposes this covariance matrix into U, S, and V
• U has shape n × n and contains eigenvectors or principal directions
• The first k columns of U form U_reduce with shape n × k

question.de: How are projection and reconstruction computed in PCA?
question.en: How are projection and reconstruction computed in PCA?

answer.de:
• Select U_reduce as the first k columns of U
• Project the data by computing Z = X · U_reduce
• Z has shape m × k and contains the reduced representation
• Reconstruct approximately by computing X_approx = Z · U_reduceᵀ
• Reconstruction is lossy when k is smaller than the original number of features
answer.en:
• Select U_reduce as the first k columns of U
• Project the data by computing Z = X · U_reduce
• Z has shape m × k and contains the reduced representation
• Reconstruct approximately by computing X_approx = Z · U_reduceᵀ
• Reconstruction is lossy when k is smaller than the original number of features

question.de: What does the average squared approximation error measure in PCA?
question.en: What does the average squared approximation error measure in PCA?

answer.de:
• It measures how far the reconstructed data X_approx is from the original data X
• A smaller error means the selected components preserve more information
• It is compared with total data variation to estimate retained variance
• A typical target is to retain about 90–99% of variance
• The final choice of k should also consider interpretability and downstream performance
answer.en:
• It measures how far the reconstructed data X_approx is from the original data X
• A smaller error means the selected components preserve more information
• It is compared with total data variation to estimate retained variance
• A typical target is to retain about 90–99% of variance
• The final choice of k should also consider interpretability and downstream performance

question.de: Why is homoscedasticity important in linear regression?
question.en: Why is homoscedasticity important in linear regression?

answer.de:
• Homoscedasticity means the error spread is roughly constant across input values
• If error variance grows or shrinks systematically, uncertainty estimates become unreliable
• A fan-shaped residual plot suggests heteroscedasticity
• The model may still produce predictions, but inference and confidence in coefficients can be problematic
• Transformations or different models may be needed
answer.en:
• Homoscedasticity means the error spread is roughly constant across input values
• If error variance grows or shrinks systematically, uncertainty estimates become unreliable
• A fan-shaped residual plot suggests heteroscedasticity
• The model may still produce predictions, but inference and confidence in coefficients can be problematic
• Transformations or different models may be needed

question.de: Why is autocorrelation of errors especially problematic in time-based regression data?
question.en: Why is autocorrelation of errors especially problematic in time-based regression data?

answer.de:
• Autocorrelation means errors are correlated across time or sequence order
• It violates the assumption that residuals are independent
• The model may systematically miss temporal structure
• Evaluation can become overly optimistic if nearby points are split randomly
• Time-aware models or time-respecting validation may be required
answer.en:
• Autocorrelation means errors are correlated across time or sequence order
• It violates the assumption that residuals are independent
• The model may systematically miss temporal structure
• Evaluation can become overly optimistic if nearby points are split randomly
• Time-aware models or time-respecting validation may be required

question.de: How can outliers distort a linear regression line?
question.en: How can outliers distort a linear regression line?

answer.de:
• Squared-error loss gives large residuals disproportionately high influence
• A single extreme point can pull the slope or intercept toward itself
• This can worsen predictions for the majority of normal observations
• Outliers should be checked to decide whether they are errors, rare valid cases, or important anomalies
• Robust methods or transformations may be considered
answer.en:
• Squared-error loss gives large residuals disproportionately high influence
• A single extreme point can pull the slope or intercept toward itself
• This can worsen predictions for the majority of normal observations
• Outliers should be checked to decide whether they are errors, rare valid cases, or important anomalies
• Robust methods or transformations may be considered

question.de: What happens in gradient descent if the parameters are exactly at a local minimum?
question.en: What happens in gradient descent if the parameters are exactly at a local minimum?

answer.de:
• The gradient is zero or approximately zero
• The update step becomes zero or very small
• The parameters stay unchanged unless noise or another optimization mechanism moves them
• For convex linear regression cost, local and global minimum coincide
• For non-convex models, a local minimum may not be the best possible solution
answer.en:
• The gradient is zero or approximately zero
• The update step becomes zero or very small
• The parameters stay unchanged unless noise or another optimization mechanism moves them
• For convex linear regression cost, local and global minimum coincide
• For non-convex models, a local minimum may not be the best possible solution

question.de: Why is the intercept term usually not regularized?
question.en: Why is the intercept term usually not regularized?

answer.de:
• The intercept controls the baseline prediction when features are zero or centered
• Penalizing it does not reduce feature-driven model complexity in the same way
• Regularizing the intercept can unnecessarily shift all predictions
• Ridge and Lasso usually penalize feature coefficients but exclude θ₀
• This keeps shrinkage focused on predictor effects
answer.en:
• The intercept controls the baseline prediction when features are zero or centered
• Penalizing it does not reduce feature-driven model complexity in the same way
• Regularizing the intercept can unnecessarily shift all predictions
• Ridge and Lasso usually penalize feature coefficients but exclude θ₀
• This keeps shrinkage focused on predictor effects

question.de: Why can Elastic Net be preferred when predictors are correlated?
question.en: Why can Elastic Net be preferred when predictors are correlated?

answer.de:
• Elastic Net combines L1 and L2 penalties
• The L1 part can produce sparsity and feature selection
• The L2 part stabilizes coefficient estimates when predictors are correlated
• Pure Lasso may arbitrarily select one variable from a correlated group
• Elastic Net can keep groups of related predictors more reliably
answer.en:
• Elastic Net combines L1 and L2 penalties
• The L1 part can produce sparsity and feature selection
• The L2 part stabilizes coefficient estimates when predictors are correlated
• Pure Lasso may arbitrarily select one variable from a correlated group
• Elastic Net can keep groups of related predictors more reliably

question.de: How does log loss penalize confident wrong predictions?
question.en: How does log loss penalize confident wrong predictions?

answer.de:
• For y = 1, the loss contains −log(hθ(x))
• If the model predicts a probability close to 0 for a true positive, the loss becomes very large
• For y = 0, the loss contains −log(1 − hθ(x))
• If the model predicts a probability close to 1 for a true negative, the loss becomes very large
• This encourages calibrated probabilities and strongly discourages confident mistakes
answer.en:
• For y = 1, the loss contains −log(hθ(x))
• If the model predicts a probability close to 0 for a true positive, the loss becomes very large
• For y = 0, the loss contains −log(1 − hθ(x))
• If the model predicts a probability close to 1 for a true negative, the loss becomes very large
• This encourages calibrated probabilities and strongly discourages confident mistakes

question.de: Why can adding polynomial features make logistic regression nonlinear in the original input space but still linear in parameters?
question.en: Why can adding polynomial features make logistic regression nonlinear in the original input space but still linear in parameters?

answer.de:
• The model remains a weighted sum of features and coefficients
• New features such as x₁², x₂², or x₁x₂ are transformations of the original inputs
• The decision boundary is linear in the transformed feature space
• In the original feature space, this can appear as a curve or more complex boundary
• Regularization becomes important because many polynomial features increase variance
answer.en:
• The model remains a weighted sum of features and coefficients
• New features such as x₁², x₂², or x₁x₂ are transformations of the original inputs
• The decision boundary is linear in the transformed feature space
• In the original feature space, this can appear as a curve or more complex boundary
• Regularization becomes important because many polynomial features increase variance

question.de: Why is 0.5 not always the best classification threshold in logistic regression?
question.en: Why is 0.5 not always the best classification threshold in logistic regression?

answer.de:
• The 0.5 threshold assumes equal costs and balanced priorities between classes
• In imbalanced or high-stakes problems, false positives and false negatives may have different costs
• Lowering the threshold can increase recall but may reduce precision
• Raising the threshold can increase precision but may reduce recall
• The threshold should be chosen using validation data and task-specific costs
answer.en:
• The 0.5 threshold assumes equal costs and balanced priorities between classes
• In imbalanced or high-stakes problems, false positives and false negatives may have different costs
• Lowering the threshold can increase recall but may reduce precision
• Raising the threshold can increase precision but may reduce recall
• The threshold should be chosen using validation data and task-specific costs

question.de: Why should softmax be preferred over independent sigmoid outputs for mutually exclusive classes?
question.en: Why should softmax be preferred over independent sigmoid outputs for mutually exclusive classes?

answer.de:
• Softmax produces a single probability distribution over all classes
• The probabilities sum to 1, so classes compete with each other
• This matches problems where exactly one class is correct
• Independent sigmoids can assign high probability to multiple classes at the same time
• Independent sigmoids are more appropriate for multi-label classification, not mutually exclusive multiclass classification
answer.en:
• Softmax produces a single probability distribution over all classes
• The probabilities sum to 1, so classes compete with each other
• This matches problems where exactly one class is correct
• Independent sigmoids can assign high probability to multiple classes at the same time
• Independent sigmoids are more appropriate for multi-label classification, not mutually exclusive multiclass classification

question.de: Why does Naive Bayes use different probability models for continuous and discrete features?
question.en: Why does Naive Bayes use different probability models for continuous and discrete features?

answer.de:
• Continuous numerical features cannot be counted directly for every possible value
• Gaussian Naive Bayes assumes continuous features follow a normal distribution within each class
• Discrete or count-based features can be modeled with multinomial probabilities
• The probability model must match the feature type
• A wrong distributional assumption can reduce probability estimates and classification quality
answer.en:
• Continuous numerical features cannot be counted directly for every possible value
• Gaussian Naive Bayes assumes continuous features follow a normal distribution within each class
• Discrete or count-based features can be modeled with multinomial probabilities
• The probability model must match the feature type
• A wrong distributional assumption can reduce probability estimates and classification quality

question.de: Why is the equal-importance assumption of Naive Bayes potentially unrealistic?
question.en: Why is the equal-importance assumption of Naive Bayes potentially unrealistic?

answer.de:
• Naive Bayes does not learn flexible feature weights like logistic regression
• Each feature contributes through its estimated class-conditional probability
• Highly informative and weak features are not explicitly reweighted by a discriminative objective
• Correlated features may effectively be counted more than once
• This can distort posterior probabilities even when class predictions are sometimes still correct
answer.en:
• Naive Bayes does not learn flexible feature weights like logistic regression
• Each feature contributes through its estimated class-conditional probability
• Highly informative and weak features are not explicitly reweighted by a discriminative objective
• Correlated features may effectively be counted more than once
• This can distort posterior probabilities even when class predictions are sometimes still correct

question.de: What is the difference between a binary split and a multi-way split in a decision tree?
question.en: What is the difference between a binary split and a multi-way split in a decision tree?

answer.de:
• A binary split divides data into exactly two child nodes
• A multi-way split creates one branch for each category or interval group
• Multi-way splits can fit categorical data naturally but may create many small branches
• Many branches can increase overfitting risk
• Gain ratio was introduced partly to reduce preference for splits with many branches
answer.en:
• A binary split divides data into exactly two child nodes
• A multi-way split creates one branch for each category or interval group
• Multi-way splits can fit categorical data naturally but may create many small branches
• Many branches can increase overfitting risk
• Gain ratio was introduced partly to reduce preference for splits with many branches

question.de: Why can information gain favor attributes with many distinct values, and how does gain ratio address this?
question.en: Why can information gain favor attributes with many distinct values, and how does gain ratio address this?

answer.de:
• Information gain may prefer features that create many small or pure partitions
• A feature like an ID can appear highly informative but generalize poorly
• Gain ratio divides information gain by split entropy
• This penalizes splits that fragment the data excessively
• It helps reduce overfitting caused by overly specific branches
answer.en:
• Information gain may prefer features that create many small or pure partitions
• A feature like an ID can appear highly informative but generalize poorly
• Gain ratio divides information gain by split entropy
• This penalizes splits that fragment the data excessively
• It helps reduce overfitting caused by overly specific branches

question.de: Why can single decision trees perform poorly when the true class boundary is not rectangular?
question.en: Why can single decision trees perform poorly when the true class boundary is not rectangular?

answer.de:
• Decision trees split one feature at a time
• This partitions the feature space into axis-aligned rectangular regions
• If the real boundary is diagonal, circular, or smooth, many splits may be required
• A deep tree can approximate the boundary but may overfit
• Ensembles often improve performance by combining many such partitions
answer.en:
• Decision trees split one feature at a time
• This partitions the feature space into axis-aligned rectangular regions
• If the real boundary is diagonal, circular, or smooth, many splits may be required
• A deep tree can approximate the boundary but may overfit
• Ensembles often improve performance by combining many such partitions

question.de: What two objectives are balanced in a soft-margin SVM?
question.en: What two objectives are balanced in a soft-margin SVM?

answer.de:
• The SVM tries to maximize the margin between classes
• It also tries to minimize classification or margin violations
• The C parameter controls the penalty for misclassified or violating examples
• Large C emphasizes fitting training data more strictly
• Small C allows more violations to favor a wider margin and generalization
answer.en:
• The SVM tries to maximize the margin between classes
• It also tries to minimize classification or margin violations
• The C parameter controls the penalty for misclassified or violating examples
• Large C emphasizes fitting training data more strictly
• Small C allows more violations to favor a wider margin and generalization

question.de: Why does a very small sigma in the RBF kernel increase overfitting risk?
question.en: Why does a very small sigma in the RBF kernel increase overfitting risk?

answer.de:
• Small sigma makes similarity decrease very quickly with distance
• Each training point affects only a very local region
• The decision boundary can become highly irregular
• This reduces bias but increases variance
• Validation performance should be used to tune sigma or gamma
answer.en:
• Small sigma makes similarity decrease very quickly with distance
• Each training point affects only a very local region
• The decision boundary can become highly irregular
• This reduces bias but increases variance
• Validation performance should be used to tune sigma or gamma

question.de: What failure can occur if RBF SVM hyperparameters are tuned on unscaled features?
question.en: What failure can occur if RBF SVM hyperparameters are tuned on unscaled features?

answer.de:
• The RBF kernel computes similarity from distances between examples
• An unscaled high-range feature can dominate those distances
• Gamma or sigma may be tuned to compensate for measurement units instead of real structure
• The resulting boundary may ignore smaller-scale but important features
• Scaling should be fitted on the training data and then applied consistently to validation and test data
answer.en:
• The RBF kernel computes similarity from distances between examples
• An unscaled high-range feature can dominate those distances
• Gamma or sigma may be tuned to compensate for measurement units instead of real structure
• The resulting boundary may ignore smaller-scale but important features
• Scaling should be fitted on the training data and then applied consistently to validation and test data

question.de: How can the same confusion matrix reveal different priorities depending on the application?
question.en: How can the same confusion matrix reveal different priorities depending on the application?

answer.de:
• In medical screening, false negatives may be most dangerous because sick patients are missed
• In spam filtering, false positives may be costly because legitimate emails are blocked
• Precision focuses on reliability of positive predictions
• Recall focuses on detecting actual positives
• The same model can be acceptable in one domain and unacceptable in another
answer.en:
• In medical screening, false negatives may be most dangerous because sick patients are missed
• In spam filtering, false positives may be costly because legitimate emails are blocked
• Precision focuses on reliability of positive predictions
• Recall focuses on detecting actual positives
• The same model can be acceptable in one domain and unacceptable in another

question.de: Why is balanced accuracy useful when classes are imbalanced?
question.en: Why is balanced accuracy useful when classes are imbalanced?

answer.de:
• It averages recall across classes instead of counting all examples equally
• Majority-class performance does not dominate the metric as strongly as in accuracy
• It reveals whether the minority class is being ignored
• It is useful when both classes should contribute equally to evaluation
• It should still be interpreted together with the confusion matrix
answer.en:
• It averages recall across classes instead of counting all examples equally
• Majority-class performance does not dominate the metric as strongly as in accuracy
• It reveals whether the minority class is being ignored
• It is useful when both classes should contribute equally to evaluation
• It should still be interpreted together with the confusion matrix

question.de: What are the risks of random undersampling and random oversampling?
question.en: What are the risks of random undersampling and random oversampling?

answer.de:
• Random undersampling can remove useful majority-class information
• This may make the decision boundary less reliable
• Random oversampling duplicates minority examples
• Duplicates can cause the model to memorize minority cases and overfit
• More careful methods can preserve information or create more diverse synthetic samples
answer.en:
• Random undersampling can remove useful majority-class information
• This may make the decision boundary less reliable
• Random oversampling duplicates minority examples
• Duplicates can cause the model to memorize minority cases and overfit
• More careful methods can preserve information or create more diverse synthetic samples

question.de: What makes bootstrap sampling different from ordinary random splitting?
question.en: What makes bootstrap sampling different from ordinary random splitting?

answer.de:
• Bootstrap samples are drawn with replacement
• The bootstrap training sample has the same size as the original dataset
• Some examples appear multiple times
• Some examples are not selected and become out-of-bag cases
• Small training sets can suffer from bias because repeated examples reduce effective diversity
answer.en:
• Bootstrap samples are drawn with replacement
• The bootstrap training sample has the same size as the original dataset
• Some examples appear multiple times
• Some examples are not selected and become out-of-bag cases
• Small training sets can suffer from bias because repeated examples reduce effective diversity

question.de: When is leave-one-out cross-validation attractive, and what is its drawback?
question.en: When is leave-one-out cross-validation attractive, and what is its drawback?

answer.de:
• It is attractive when the dataset is very small
• Each training run uses N−1 examples, so almost all available data is used for training
• Every example is tested once
• It can be computationally expensive because N models must be trained
• It can also have high variance in some settings
answer.en:
• It is attractive when the dataset is very small
• Each training run uses N−1 examples, so almost all available data is used for training
• Every example is tested once
• It can be computationally expensive because N models must be trained
• It can also have high variance in some settings

question.de: Why can choosing the numerically best validation score lead to an overly complex model?
question.en: Why can choosing the numerically best validation score lead to an overly complex model?

answer.de:
• Validation scores contain noise from the particular data split
• A complex model may win by chance on the validation set
• Small differences in score may not be practically meaningful
• Simpler models are often preferred when performance is similar
• This reduces overfitting risk and improves interpretability
answer.en:
• Validation scores contain noise from the particular data split
• A complex model may win by chance on the validation set
• Small differences in score may not be practically meaningful
• Simpler models are often preferred when performance is similar
• This reduces overfitting risk and improves interpretability

question.de: How can learning curves show that the validation set is not representative?
question.en: How can learning curves show that the validation set is not representative?

answer.de:
• Training and validation curves may show unstable or unusual gaps
• Validation error may fluctuate strongly as more data is added
• Validation performance may be much better or worse than expected from training behavior
• This can happen if validation data has a different distribution
• The split strategy, stratification, or data collection process should be checked
answer.en:
• Training and validation curves may show unstable or unusual gaps
• Validation error may fluctuate strongly as more data is added
• Validation performance may be much better or worse than expected from training behavior
• This can happen if validation data has a different distribution
• The split strategy, stratification, or data collection process should be checked

question.de: What does the No-Free-Lunch theorem imply for exam-style model choice questions?
question.en: What does the No-Free-Lunch theorem imply for exam-style model choice questions?

answer.de:
• There is no universally best learning algorithm
• A method that works well on one data distribution may fail on another
• Model choice must depend on the task, data, metric, constraints, and assumptions
• Empirical comparison using validation or cross-validation is necessary
• Strong exam answers should justify why the chosen model matches the problem
answer.en:
• There is no universally best learning algorithm
• A method that works well on one data distribution may fail on another
• Model choice must depend on the task, data, metric, constraints, and assumptions
• Empirical comparison using validation or cross-validation is necessary
• Strong exam answers should justify why the chosen model matches the problem

question.de: Why are bagging models easy to parallelize but boosting models are not?
question.en: Why are bagging models easy to parallelize but boosting models are not?

answer.de:
• In bagging, base models are trained independently on different bootstrap samples
• Each model does not need the previous model’s errors
• Therefore, many models can be trained at the same time
• In boosting, each model depends on reweighted errors from earlier models
• This sequential dependency makes boosting harder to parallelize
answer.en:
• In bagging, base models are trained independently on different bootstrap samples
• Each model does not need the previous model’s errors
• Therefore, many models can be trained at the same time
• In boosting, each model depends on reweighted errors from earlier models
• This sequential dependency makes boosting harder to parallelize

question.de: How does weighted voting in boosting differ from majority voting in bagging?
question.en: How does weighted voting in boosting differ from majority voting in bagging?

answer.de:
• Bagging typically gives each base model an equal vote
• Boosting weights model predictions according to model performance or error
• More accurate weak learners can contribute more to the final decision
• Misclassified samples influence later models during training
• The final ensemble is therefore both sequential and weighted
answer.en:
• Bagging typically gives each base model an equal vote
• Boosting weights model predictions according to model performance or error
• More accurate weak learners can contribute more to the final decision
• Misclassified samples influence later models during training
• The final ensemble is therefore both sequential and weighted

question.de: Why should two independent labelers be used in a data-centric workflow?
question.en: Why should two independent labelers be used in a data-centric workflow?

answer.de:
• Independent labels allow measurement of annotation consistency
• Disagreements reveal ambiguous definitions or unclear instructions
• The goal is to improve the labeling guideline, not only to blame labelers
• Consistent labels make the learning target clearer
• This can improve model performance without changing the algorithm
answer.en:
• Independent labels allow measurement of annotation consistency
• Disagreements reveal ambiguous definitions or unclear instructions
• The goal is to improve the labeling guideline, not only to blame labelers
• Consistent labels make the learning target clearer
• This can improve model performance without changing the algorithm

question.de: What does “good data” mean in the data-centric AI view?
question.en: What does “good data” mean in the data-centric AI view?

answer.de:
• Labels are defined consistently and unambiguously
• Important input cases are covered sufficiently
• The dataset contains representative examples for relevant conditions
• Feedback from production data is available over time
• The data distribution is monitored for drift and changing conditions
answer.en:
• Labels are defined consistently and unambiguously
• Important input cases are covered sufficiently
• The dataset contains representative examples for relevant conditions
• Feedback from production data is available over time
• The data distribution is monitored for drift and changing conditions

question.de: Why should model monitoring separate data-quality bugs from true drift?
question.en: Why should model monitoring separate data-quality bugs from true drift?

answer.de:
• Performance drops can be caused by broken pipelines, missing features, or corrupted inputs
• Such failures require engineering fixes, not necessarily retraining
• True data drift means the input distribution has changed
• True concept drift means the input-output relationship has changed
• Diagnosing the cause prevents inappropriate or harmful updates
answer.en:
• Performance drops can be caused by broken pipelines, missing features, or corrupted inputs
• Such failures require engineering fixes, not necessarily retraining
• True data drift means the input distribution has changed
• True concept drift means the input-output relationship has changed
• Diagnosing the cause prevents inappropriate or harmful updates

question.de: How do you determine the number and size of weight matrices in a fully connected neural network?
question.en: How do you determine the number and size of weight matrices in a fully connected neural network?

answer.de:
• There is one weight matrix between each pair of consecutive layers
• Rows usually correspond to units in the next layer
• Columns correspond to units in the previous layer plus a bias input
• Example: 3 inputs plus bias to 4 hidden units gives a 4 × 4 matrix
• The exact convention can vary, but bias units must be included when counting parameters
answer.en:
• There is one weight matrix between each pair of consecutive layers
• Rows usually correspond to units in the next layer
• Columns correspond to units in the previous layer plus a bias input
• Example: 3 inputs plus bias to 4 hidden units gives a 4 × 4 matrix
• The exact convention can vary, but bias units must be included when counting parameters

question.de: Why is the bias unit added separately from ordinary input features?
question.en: Why is the bias unit added separately from ordinary input features?

answer.de:
• The bias lets a neuron shift its activation threshold
• Without bias, the neuron’s boundary may be forced through the origin in transformed space
• Bias behaves like a constant input equal to 1
• It has a trainable weight like other inputs
• Bias improves flexibility even when all actual feature values are zero
answer.en:
• The bias lets a neuron shift its activation threshold
• Without bias, the neuron’s boundary may be forced through the origin in transformed space
• Bias behaves like a constant input equal to 1
• It has a trainable weight like other inputs
• Bias improves flexibility even when all actual feature values are zero

question.de: Why can random Gaussian initialization with large variance cause training problems?
question.en: Why can random Gaussian initialization with large variance cause training problems?

answer.de:
• Large random weights can push activations into saturated regions
• Saturated sigmoid or tanh units produce very small gradients
• Gradients may vanish during backpropagation
• Training becomes slow or unstable
• Xavier, Glorot, or LeCun-style initializations aim to keep activation and gradient scales controlled
answer.en:
• Large random weights can push activations into saturated regions
• Saturated sigmoid or tanh units produce very small gradients
• Gradients may vanish during backpropagation
• Training becomes slow or unstable
• Xavier, Glorot, or LeCun-style initializations aim to keep activation and gradient scales controlled

question.de: What is the intuition behind gradient descent with momentum?
question.en: What is the intuition behind gradient descent with momentum?

answer.de:
• Momentum adds part of the previous update direction to the current update
• It can accelerate movement along consistent downhill directions
• It can smooth noisy updates from mini-batches
• It may slow oscillations near narrow valleys
• The analogy is a ball gaining speed on slopes and slowing near minima
answer.en:
• Momentum adds part of the previous update direction to the current update
• It can accelerate movement along consistent downhill directions
• It can smooth noisy updates from mini-batches
• It may slow oscillations near narrow valleys
• The analogy is a ball gaining speed on slopes and slowing near minima

question.de: Why must the neural-network output layer match the prediction task?
question.en: Why must the neural-network output layer match the prediction task?

answer.de:
• Binary classification usually needs one sigmoid output
• Multiclass classification usually needs K outputs with softmax
• Regression usually needs a linear output without probability normalization
• A mismatch between output activation and target type makes the loss interpretation wrong
• The output design determines how predictions are decoded
answer.en:
• Binary classification usually needs one sigmoid output
• Multiclass classification usually needs K outputs with softmax
• Regression usually needs a linear output without probability normalization
• A mismatch between output activation and target type makes the loss interpretation wrong
• The output design determines how predictions are decoded

question.de: How do you compute the spatial output size of a convolution or pooling operation?
question.en: How do you compute the spatial output size of a convolution or pooling operation?

answer.de:
• For input size n, filter size f, padding p, and stride s, output size is floor((n + 2p − f) / s + 1)
• If the result is not an integer, the lower integer is used
• Larger padding increases output size
• Larger stride decreases output size
• The same logic is used for convolution and pooling spatial dimensions
answer.en:
• For input size n, filter size f, padding p, and stride s, output size is floor((n + 2p − f) / s + 1)
• If the result is not an integer, the lower integer is used
• Larger padding increases output size
• Larger stride decreases output size
• The same logic is used for convolution and pooling spatial dimensions

question.de: How many trainable parameters are in a convolutional layer with 10 filters of size 3 × 3 × 3?
question.en: How many trainable parameters are in a convolutional layer with 10 filters of size 3 × 3 × 3?

answer.de:
• Each filter has 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 weights
• Each filter also has one bias parameter
• One filter therefore has 28 parameters
• With 10 filters, the layer has 28 × 10 = 280 parameters
• The number of spatial positions does not increase the number of filter parameters because weights are shared
answer.en:
• Each filter has 3 × 3 × 3 = 27 weights
• Each filter also has one bias parameter
• One filter therefore has 28 parameters
• With 10 filters, the layer has 28 × 10 = 280 parameters
• The number of spatial positions does not increase the number of filter parameters because weights are shared

question.de: Why does convolution without padding lose border information?
question.en: Why does convolution without padding lose border information?

answer.de:
• A filter can only be centered where it fully fits inside the input
• Corner pixels are used fewer times than central pixels
• Repeated convolution layers shrink the feature map
• Information near the image boundary may disappear quickly
• Padding reduces shrinkage and makes border pixels participate more often
answer.en:
• A filter can only be centered where it fully fits inside the input
• Corner pixels are used fewer times than central pixels
• Repeated convolution layers shrink the feature map
• Information near the image boundary may disappear quickly
• Padding reduces shrinkage and makes border pixels participate more often

question.de: Why must a convolutional filter for an RGB image have depth 3?
question.en: Why must a convolutional filter for an RGB image have depth 3?

answer.de:
• RGB images have three channels: red, green, and blue
• The filter must span the full depth of the input volume
• A 3 × 3 filter on an RGB image is therefore 3 × 3 × 3
• The convolution combines spatial and channel information
• Multiple filters produce multiple output feature maps
answer.en:
• RGB images have three channels: red, green, and blue
• The filter must span the full depth of the input volume
• A 3 × 3 filter on an RGB image is therefore 3 × 3 × 3
• The convolution combines spatial and channel information
• Multiple filters produce multiple output feature maps

question.de: What architectural trend is common in classical CNNs such as LeNet, AlexNet, and VGG?
question.en: What architectural trend is common in classical CNNs such as LeNet, AlexNet, and VGG?

answer.de:
• Early layers process large spatial maps with relatively few channels
• Deeper layers usually have smaller height and width
• The number of channels often increases with depth
• Early layers detect simple features such as edges
• Deeper layers detect more complex object parts or semantic patterns
answer.en:
• Early layers process large spatial maps with relatively few channels
• Deeper layers usually have smaller height and width
• The number of channels often increases with depth
• Early layers detect simple features such as edges
• Deeper layers detect more complex object parts or semantic patterns

question.de: What is negative transfer in transfer learning?
question.en: What is negative transfer in transfer learning?

answer.de:
• Negative transfer occurs when reused representations hurt performance on the target task
• It can happen when source and target domains are too different
• The pretrained model may transfer irrelevant biases or features
• Over-freezing can prevent adaptation, while over-fine-tuning can destroy useful pretrained features
• Transfer learning works best when source and target distributions are similar enough
answer.en:
• Negative transfer occurs when reused representations hurt performance on the target task
• It can happen when source and target domains are too different
• The pretrained model may transfer irrelevant biases or features
• Over-freezing can prevent adaptation, while over-fine-tuning can destroy useful pretrained features
• Transfer learning works best when source and target distributions are similar enough

question.de: Why must the sampling interval be sufficiently small in time series data?
question.en: Why must the sampling interval be sufficiently small in time series data?

answer.de:
• A large sampling interval can miss important changes between observations
• Interpolation becomes less reliable if the signal changes faster than the sampling rate
• Key peaks, cycles, or anomalies may disappear
• Forecasting models then learn an incomplete representation of the process
• The sampling interval should match the dynamics of the phenomenon
answer.en:
• A large sampling interval can miss important changes between observations
• Interpolation becomes less reliable if the signal changes faster than the sampling rate
• Key peaks, cycles, or anomalies may disappear
• Forecasting models then learn an incomplete representation of the process
• The sampling interval should match the dynamics of the phenomenon

question.de: Why is stationarity important for classical time series forecasting?
question.en: Why is stationarity important for classical time series forecasting?

answer.de:
• Stationarity means statistical properties such as mean, variance, and autocorrelation remain stable over time
• Many classical models assume stable structure
• Non-stationary trend or seasonality can make forecasts unreliable
• Differencing in ARIMA is used to help handle non-stationarity
• Stationarity simplifies analysis and improves reliability of assumptions
answer.en:
• Stationarity means statistical properties such as mean, variance, and autocorrelation remain stable over time
• Many classical models assume stable structure
• Non-stationary trend or seasonality can make forecasts unreliable
• Differencing in ARIMA is used to help handle non-stationarity
• Stationarity simplifies analysis and improves reliability of assumptions

question.de: When is a seasonal naive forecast more appropriate than a naive forecast?
question.en: When is a seasonal naive forecast more appropriate than a naive forecast?

answer.de:
• A naive forecast repeats the last observed value
• A seasonal naive forecast repeats the value from the corresponding previous season
• It is appropriate when the dominant pattern is seasonal
• Example: predicting Monday demand from the previous Monday can be better than using Sunday
• It is a strong baseline for seasonal time series
answer.en:
• A naive forecast repeats the last observed value
• A seasonal naive forecast repeats the value from the corresponding previous season
• It is appropriate when the dominant pattern is seasonal
• Example: predicting Monday demand from the previous Monday can be better than using Sunday
• It is a strong baseline for seasonal time series

question.de: What do the p, d, and q parameters represent in ARIMA(p,d,q)?
question.en: What do the p, d, and q parameters represent in ARIMA(p,d,q)?

answer.de:
• p is the number of autoregressive lags
• d is the order of differencing used to reduce non-stationarity
• q is the number of moving-average error lags
• AR terms use past values of the series
• MA terms use past forecasting errors
answer.en:
• p is the number of autoregressive lags
• d is the order of differencing used to reduce non-stationarity
• q is the number of moving-average error lags
• AR terms use past values of the series
• MA terms use past forecasting errors

question.de: Why is backpropagation through time more resource-intensive than ordinary backpropagation?
question.en: Why is backpropagation through time more resource-intensive than ordinary backpropagation?

answer.de:
• The recurrent model is unfolded across all time steps
• Gradients must be propagated backward through the sequence
• Long sequences create many repeated computations
• Memory is needed to store intermediate states
• This increases computational cost and can create vanishing or exploding gradients
answer.en:
• The recurrent model is unfolded across all time steps
• Gradients must be propagated backward through the sequence
• Long sequences create many repeated computations
• Memory is needed to store intermediate states
• This increases computational cost and can create vanishing or exploding gradients

question.de: How do the forget, update/input, and output gates of an LSTM control memory?
question.en: How do the forget, update/input, and output gates of an LSTM control memory?

answer.de:
• The forget gate decides which old cell-state information should be kept or erased
• The update/input gate decides which new candidate information should be written into memory
• The output gate decides how much of the cell state becomes visible as the hidden state
• Gate values come from sigmoid activations between 0 and 1
• This selective flow helps preserve long-term dependencies
answer.en:
• The forget gate decides which old cell-state information should be kept or erased
• The update/input gate decides which new candidate information should be written into memory
• The output gate decides how much of the cell state becomes visible as the hidden state
• Gate values come from sigmoid activations between 0 and 1
• This selective flow helps preserve long-term dependencies

question.de: What does it mean if a GRU update gate is close to 0 or close to 1?
question.en: What does it mean if a GRU update gate is close to 0 or close to 1?

answer.de:
• The update gate controls how much the memory changes at the current step
• A value close to 0 keeps much of the previous memory
• A value close to 1 writes more of the new candidate information
• This lets the GRU preserve information across many words or time steps
• It reduces the short-term-memory limitation of standard RNNs
answer.en:
• The update gate controls how much the memory changes at the current step
• A value close to 0 keeps much of the previous memory
• A value close to 1 writes more of the new candidate information
• This lets the GRU preserve information across many words or time steps
• It reduces the short-term-memory limitation of standard RNNs

question.de: Why is choosing an anomaly threshold not only a statistical decision?
question.en: Why is choosing an anomaly threshold not only a statistical decision?

answer.de:
• The threshold controls the trade-off between missed anomalies and false alarms
• A low threshold detects more events but may overwhelm users with alerts
• A high threshold reduces false alarms but may miss important failures
• The acceptable balance depends on domain costs and actionability
• Expert review and operational constraints are needed
answer.en:
• The threshold controls the trade-off between missed anomalies and false alarms
• A low threshold detects more events but may overwhelm users with alerts
• A high threshold reduces false alarms but may miss important failures
• The acceptable balance depends on domain costs and actionability
• Expert review and operational constraints are needed

question.de: How do mean and standard deviation shape Gaussian anomaly detection?
question.en: How do mean and standard deviation shape Gaussian anomaly detection?

answer.de:
• The mean μ defines the center of normal behavior
• The standard deviation σ defines the spread of normal variation
• Points far from μ relative to σ have lower probability density
• A wider σ makes extreme values seem less surprising
• A narrow σ makes deviations easier to flag
answer.en:
• The mean μ defines the center of normal behavior
• The standard deviation σ defines the spread of normal variation
• Points far from μ relative to σ have lower probability density
• A wider σ makes extreme values seem less surprising
• A narrow σ makes deviations easier to flag

question.de: Why should detected anomalies be analyzed instead of automatically removed?
question.en: Why should detected anomalies be analyzed instead of automatically removed?

answer.de:
• Some anomalies are data errors and should be corrected or removed
• Some anomalies are rare but valid and may be the most important cases
• Some anomalies indicate fraud, system failure, or new behavior
• Removing them blindly can erase useful information
• The response should depend on root cause and task objective
answer.en:
• Some anomalies are data errors and should be corrected or removed
• Some anomalies are rare but valid and may be the most important cases
• Some anomalies indicate fraud, system failure, or new behavior
• Removing them blindly can erase useful information
• The response should depend on root cause and task objective

question.de: Why can optimizing only for accuracy hide ethical problems?
question.en: Why can optimizing only for accuracy hide ethical problems?

answer.de:
• Overall accuracy can hide unequal error rates across groups
• A model may work well for majority groups and poorly for underrepresented groups
• Harmful false positives or false negatives may be concentrated in specific populations
• The metric encodes what the system values
• Fairness, accountability, transparency, and impact analysis must complement accuracy
answer.en:
• Overall accuracy can hide unequal error rates across groups
• A model may work well for majority groups and poorly for underrepresented groups
• Harmful false positives or false negatives may be concentrated in specific populations
• The metric encodes what the system values
• Fairness, accountability, transparency, and impact analysis must complement accuracy

question.de: Why can a technically correct AI system still be socially wrong?
question.en: Why can a technically correct AI system still be socially wrong?

answer.de:
• The model may optimize the stated metric correctly
• The problem framing itself may be harmful or incomplete
• The data may reflect historical inequality
• Deployment can change power, access, or opportunity for affected people
• Responsible AI requires technical understanding plus problem-context understanding
answer.en:
• The model may optimize the stated metric correctly
• The problem framing itself may be harmful or incomplete
• The data may reflect historical inequality
• Deployment can change power, access, or opportunity for affected people
• Responsible AI requires technical understanding plus problem-context understanding
